metadata.title: Why Helpful Feedback Can Steal the Student's Own Learning

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Aloha, Liam — in for Bear. The more complete and precise Claude's feedback is, the more it may be doing the student's revision work — leaving the student better informed but no better at revising? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The more complete and precise Claude's feedback is, the more it may be doing the student's revision work — leaving the student better informed but no better at revising?
props.segment: Why Helpful Feedback Can Steal the

beats.narration_text: The more complete and precise Claude's feedback is, the more it may be doing the student's revision work — leaving the student better informed but no better at revising.

card.copy: Why helpful feedback can steal the student's own learning

beats.narration_text: A student submits a history essay. The thesis lists causes of World War I: nationalism, militarism, the alliance system. Priya asks Claude for feedback. Claude drafts a comment that rewrites the thesis as a model of causal argument. The student incorporates it. The next essay has the same listing problem.
beats.scene_description: Close-up of a handwritten student essay on a desk, with pencil edits and a red comment in the margin. The thesis is underlined. Desaturated documentary style, newsprint treatment.
beats.image_prompt: B02, close-up of a handwritten student essay on a wooden desk, a thesis sentence underlined in pencil, red pen comments written in the margin, papers and a pencil on the desk, warm desk lamp light, editorial documentary desaturated style, flat newsprint tone, no digital devices visible
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] student essay with thesis underlined and feedback in margin

beats.narration_text: Better feedback should produce better revision skills. Here is the case where more specific feedback produced a better-looking essay but no improvement in the student's ability to revise. Why?

card.copy: More specific feedback -> better essay. Same problem on the next one. Why?

beats.narration_text: The intuition: the more complete the feedback, the more the student understands what was wrong, and the better they do next time. Specific correction should predict skill improvement.

production_viz.label: naive chain: specific feedback -> student understands -> skill improves
production_viz.mechanic: Three TEAL blocks in a chain: 'SPECIFIC FEEDBACK', 'STUDENT UNDERSTANDS', 'SKILL IMPROVES'. Each appears with an arrow connecting it.

beats.narration_text: But revision skill is built by doing the revision thinking — by encountering the gap between your draft and the standard, and working to close it yourself. A comment that closes the gap for you removes the very cognitive labor that would have built the skill.

production_viz.label: directive feedback closes the gap — for the student, not by the student
production_viz.mechanic: A student's draft block (CRIMSON-border) on the left. A standard block (TEAL-border) on the right. A wide gap between them. A 'DIRECTIVE FEEDBACK' label drops in and a CRIMSON bridge slides across the gap — closing it FROM OUTSIDE. A serif label: 'gap closed by feedback, not by student'.
production_viz.note: the key distinction: who closes the gap

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] CRIMSON bridge closes the gap from outside

beats.narration_text: There are three types of feedback response. Type One describes what is wrong. Type Two asks a question that makes the student think. Type Three tells the student what to change — or rewrites it for them.

card.copy: Three types: describe, ask, or rewrite

beats.narration_text: Claude's default is Type Three. Priya asks for feedback on the WWI thesis. Claude Draft A rewrites it: 'Revise your thesis to argue nationalism was the primary trigger, with the alliance system as its amplifier.' The student incorporates it. The next draft has the same listing problem — because the student never constructed the argument.

production_viz.label: Type 3 feedback — rewrite supplied
production_viz.mechanic: A feedback card appears in CRIMSON-border. Three lines of text fade in: 'STUDENT THESIS: lists nationalism, militarism, alliance system.' Then 'CLAUDE DRAFT A: Revise to argue nationalism was the trigger...' Then a CRIMSON chip: 'TYPE 3 — directive rewrite'. Below: a small essay icon showing next-draft same problem.
production_viz.note: show the draft A feedback and its consequence

beats.narration_text: Claude Draft B: 'If a reader asked — so what relationship between these causes matters most? — what would you want them to understand?' That is a Type Two coaching question. The student has to construct the argument. The next draft shows causal structure Priya did not supply.

production_viz.label: Type 2 feedback — coaching question
production_viz.mechanic: A feedback card in TEAL-border. Lines fade in: 'CLAUDE DRAFT B: If a reader asked — what relationship between these causes matters most? — what would you want them to understand?' A TEAL chip: 'TYPE 2 — coaching question'. Below: a small essay icon showing the next draft with improved causal structure.
production_viz.note: show draft B and its consequence

beats.narration_text: The difference is not which essay looks better after one round. It is who did the revision thinking. Type Three produces a better draft. Type Two produces a better drafter.

card.copy: Type Three produces a better draft. Type Two produces a better drafter.
card.attribution: — the distinction that determines who learns

beats.narration_text: The practice: when asking Claude for feedback, add two constraints to your prompt. One — draft three coaching questions, not a correction. Two — do not rewrite any of the student's sentences. Those two lines stop Claude from defaulting to Type Three.

production_viz.label: the two constraints that stop overhelping
production_viz.mechanic: A prompt window card appears. Two TEAL lines write in beneath the main request: '1. Draft three coaching questions, not a correction.' '2. Do not rewrite any of the student's sentences.' A TEAL bracket labeled 'these two lines' points to both constraints.

beats.narration_text: Then you read Claude's options and pick the question that fits this student — one who is anxious needs a different question than one who is confident; one who has already revised once needs something different still. Claude generates the options. The teacher selects.

production_viz.label: Claude options -> teacher selects -> student thinks
production_viz.mechanic: Three coaching question chips appear in a column (TEAL). A TEAL ring appears around the middle one as a hand cursor highlights it. A serif label: 'teacher selects; student does the thinking'.
production_viz.note: the teacher stays in the loop

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three coaching question options, one selected

beats.narration_text: Feedback teaches when it supports the student's revision thinking, not when it replaces it. Claude can draft the options. Only the teacher knows which question opens thinking for this student.

production_viz.label: student closes the gap with coaching support
production_viz.mechanic: The B05 gap image returns — draft block and standard block with a gap. This time a TEAL bridge extends FROM THE DRAFT BLOCK (from the left, student side), crossing the gap. Label: 'student closes the gap'. A serif note: 'coaching question enables this'.
production_viz.note: the repaired version — same gap, closed from the student side

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] TEAL bridge from student side closes the gap

beats.narration_text: The revision is the teaching. Ask, don't rewrite.

card.copy: Directive correction removes the learning. Ask, don't rewrite.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Feedback teaches when it supports the student's revision thinking, not when it replaces it. Claude can draft the options. Only the teacher knows which question opens thinking for this student. The revision is the teaching. Ask, don't rewrite.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why Helpful Feedback Can Steal the Student's Own Learning] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
